questions.stem: An 18-year-old university student presents after a tonic-clonic seizure. She reports that for the past year she has had occasional jerking of her arms in the morning, especially after sleep deprivation. She has never lost consciousness during these jerks. An EEG shows generalised 4–6 Hz polyspike-and-wave activity. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The history of early morning myoclonic jerks and the characteristic EEG findings (generalised polyspike-and-wave) are classic for juvenile myoclonic epilepsy. Childhood absence epilepsy presents with absence seizures, not myoclonic jerks. Benign rolandic epilepsy is a focal epilepsy with centrotemporal spikes.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with focal epilepsy treated with carbamazepine presents with progressive confusion over two days. She is on no other medications. Serum sodium is 118 mmol/L (135–145). She is euvolemic. Urine osmolality is 400 mOsm/kg (serum 260), urine sodium 60 mmol/L. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone (SIADH) due to small cell lung cancer
questions.explanation: Carbamazepine is a common cause of hyponatremia due to SIADH-like effect, and the scenario fits (euvolemic hyponatremia, high urine osmolality, high urine sodium). The absence of other causes (no vomiting, no cancer history) makes carbamazepine most likely. Cerebral salt wasting typically occurs in neurosurgical patients.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with a known diagnosis of epilepsy is brought to the emergency department by ambulance. He has been convulsing continuously for 30 minutes. He is unresponsive. Temperature is 37.1°C. Blood glucose is normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Continuous convulsive activity lasting >5 minutes is defined as status epilepticus. The patient has known epilepsy and no fever, so febrile seizure is unlikely. Eclampsia occurs in pregnant/postpartum women.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with epilepsy on phenytoin for 10 years presents with a three-week history of unsteady gait and slurred speech. On examination he has nystagmus on lateral gaze and mild ataxia. Serum phenytoin level is 30 mg/L (therapeutic range 10–20). What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Phenytoin toxicity causes dose-dependent cerebellar signs (ataxia, nystagmus, dysarthria). The elevated level directly indicates toxicity. Cerebellar haemorrhage would be more acute/painful. Wernicke is associated with alcohol abuse and thiamine deficiency.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with idiopathic generalised epilepsy on levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily has been seizure-free for two years. She is now 32 weeks pregnant and presents after a single tonic-clonic seizure. She has been adherent to medication. Blood pressure is normal, urine protein negative. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Breakthrough seizure due to pregnancy-related pharmacokinetic changes
questions.explanation: Pregnancy decreases antiepileptic drug levels due to increased volume of distribution and clearance. Breakthrough seizures are common despite adherence. Eclampsia is unlikely with normal BP and no proteinuria. The diagnosis is a seizure due to subtherapeutic drug levels.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old man presents after a first tonic-clonic seizure. Neurological examination is normal. What is the single most appropriate investigation to identify a structural cause?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends MRI brain for adults with a first seizure to identify structural abnormalities such as tumour or cortical dysplasia. CT head is inferior for subtle lesions. EEG gives functional information but not structural.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old girl has brief episodes of staring and unresponsiveness, lasting 5–10 seconds. An EEG is performed. Which of the following findings is most likely?
options: Focal spikes in the left temporal region
questions.explanation: This pattern is pathognomonic for childhood absence epilepsy, consistent with the clinical description of typical absence seizures.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with a history of alcohol misuse presents with a first generalised tonic-clonic seizure. He is alert and haemodynamically stable. What is the single most appropriate initial investigation to identify an acute intracranial cause?
questions.explanation: In alcohol misuse, there is increased risk of subdural haemorrhage and other acute intracranial pathology. CT head is rapid and sensitive for acute bleeding. MRI is not first-line in an acute setting.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with focal epilepsy on phenytoin presents with ataxia, nystagmus, and dysarthria. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the cause of his symptoms?
questions.explanation: These are classic signs of phenytoin toxicity. Measuring the serum level confirms the diagnosis. Imaging and EEG would not be diagnostic.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old woman with a history of drug-resistant epilepsy presents with episodes that have atypical features such as rapid recovery and fluctuating nature. She has been on multiple antiepileptic drugs without benefit. Which investigation is the gold standard for differentiating epileptic seizures from psychogenic non-epileptic attacks?
questions.explanation: Continuous video-EEG monitoring allows correlation of clinical events with EEG findings, and is the gold standard for diagnosing psychogenic non-epileptic attacks (PNES). Serum prolactin is non-specific and not diagnostic.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man presents with new-onset focal seizures. MRI brain shows right temporal lobe lesion. He is otherwise fit and well. What is the most appropriate first-line monotherapy for his epilepsy?
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend lamotrigine as first-line monotherapy for focal seizures. Carbamazepine is an alternative but has more side effects and drug interactions. Valproate is not first-line for focal seizures and is teratogenic. Phenytoin has complex pharmacokinetics. Gabapentin is less effective for focal seizures.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with generalised tonic-clonic seizures has been well controlled on lamotrigine 200 mg daily for 6 months. She presents with a diffuse maculopapular rash developing over the past week. What is the best next step in management?
options: Continue lamotrigine and add an antihistamine
questions.explanation: Lamotrigine can cause a serious rash, including Stevens-Johnson syndrome. The rash mandates discontinuation. Levetiracetam is a first-line alternative for generalised tonic-clonic seizures and is safer in women of childbearing age. Valproate should be avoided due to teratogenicity and interaction with lamotrigine.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with known epilepsy (on lamotrigine) presents to the emergency department with convulsive status epilepticus lasting 30 minutes. IV access is obtained. What is the first-line drug for this condition?
questions.explanation: For established convulsive status epilepticus, the first-line treatment is IV lorazepam (0.1 mg/kg). Diazepam is an alternative but lorazepam has a longer duration of action. Phenytoin, levetiracetam, and valproate are second-line options.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man with generalised epilepsy has been on valproate 1000 mg daily for 2 years. He now reports weight gain, tremor, and hair loss. On examination, there is a fine tremor. What is the most appropriate change in medication?
questions.explanation: Valproate is associated with weight gain, tremor, and hair loss. These side effects are often unacceptable. Lamotrigine is a first-line alternative for generalised tonic-clonic seizures and has a better side effect profile. Carbamazepine and phenytoin are not first-line for generalised epilepsy and have their own side effects. Reducing dose may not resolve side effects. Propranolol treats tremor but does not address other issues.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with new-onset focal epilepsy has osteoporosis. Which antiepileptic drug is most appropriate for her?
questions.explanation: Enzyme-inducing antiepileptic drugs (e.g., carbamazepine, phenytoin) increase bone turnover and worsen osteoporosis. Lamotrigine is not enzyme-inducing and is first-line for focal seizures, making it the safest choice. Valproate and topiramate are not first-line for focal seizures and have other risks.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents to the emergency department after a first tonic-clonic seizure. She is now alert and neurologically normal. Observations are stable. What is the most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Refer to neurology for outpatient assessment
questions.explanation: NICE guidance states that adults with a first seizure should be referred to a specialist within 2 weeks; EEG is usually performed after specialist review, antiepileptics are not initiated after a single unprovoked seizure, and CT head is only indicated if there is suspicion of structural pathology (no focal signs here).

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with known epilepsy on levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily has had three brief focal seizures in the past week, without loss of consciousness. He has no side effects and drug levels are within the therapeutic range. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: In breakthrough seizures, the first step is to optimise the existing monotherapy by increasing the dose within the therapeutic range before considering polytherapy or switching agents.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with known epilepsy on lamotrigine presents to the emergency department with a prolonged tonic-clonic seizure lasting 8 minutes. She is still seizing. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
questions.explanation: IV lorazepam is first-line for status epilepticus (NICE); phenytoin and levetiracetam are second-line options after benzodiazepines, and intubation is considered if seizure persists despite two anticonvulsants.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman on carbamazepine for focal epilepsy develops a widespread maculopapular rash 2 weeks after starting the drug. She has no mucosal involvement, no fever, and no eosinophilia. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: A rash within weeks of starting an antiepileptic may indicate drug hypersensitivity or early SJS/TEN; the immediate action is to stop the offending drug and seek specialist advice. Switching to another AED without specialist input is not recommended, and continuing the drug is dangerous.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with juvenile myoclonic epilepsy on valproate has been seizure-free for 5 years. She is planning a pregnancy and asks about her medication. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer to neurology for medication review
Continue valproate and add folic acid
questions.explanation: NICE guidance emphasises that valproate should be avoided in women of childbearing potential unless no other effective treatment; any change must be made with specialist input. Immediate referral to neurology for individualised risk-benefit assessment is the priority.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man presents to the emergency department after a first witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure. He is drowsy but rousable. Observations: temperature 39°C, heart rate 110 bpm, blood pressure 130/80 mmHg. Which single clinical finding most strongly suggests a life-threatening cause requiring immediate treatment?
questions.explanation: Neck stiffness with fever in a postictal patient raises suspicion of meningitis, which requires urgent LP and antibiotics. Tongue biting and incontinence are common after seizures and do not indicate a specific life-threatening cause.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman with epilepsy started lamotrigine 6 weeks ago. She presents with a widespread pruritic rash, fever, and sore throat. On examination, there is a maculopapular rash sparing the palms and soles. Which additional finding would most strongly indicate that she may have Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) and requires immediate cessation of lamotrigine?
questions.explanation: Mucous membrane involvement (conjunctival injection, oral erosions) is a hallmark of SJS/TEN. Palatal petechiae are more typical of scarlet fever. Conjunctival injection here is a red flag for impending SJS.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with known epilepsy is brought in by ambulance after a seizure that has been ongoing for 6 minutes. He is actively convulsing. Which clinical finding confirms that he is in convulsive status epilepticus?
questions.explanation: Convulsive status epilepticus is defined as a single seizure lasting >5 minutes or repeated seizures without recovery of consciousness. Duration >5 minutes is the key diagnostic threshold.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man on phenytoin for epilepsy presents with dizziness and unsteadiness. He recently started omeprazole for dyspepsia. On examination, which single neurological sign is most suggestive of phenytoin toxicity?
questions.explanation: Horizontal nystagmus is an early and characteristic sign of phenytoin toxicity. Vertical nystagmus suggests brainstem pathology. Ataxia and dysmetria can occur but nystagmus is earlier and more specific.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with juvenile myoclonic epilepsy on lamotrigine has been seizure-free for 3 years. She wishes to discuss withdrawing her medication. Which EEG finding would be a red flag against withdrawal?
questions.explanation: In JME, the presence of generalised polyspike-wave discharges (particularly with photoparoxysmal response) indicates ongoing epileptogenicity and high risk of relapse if AEDs are withdrawn. Normal EEG or benign findings do not preclude withdrawal.
